headline: A Humble Herb Could Change Malaria Prevention in Africa
meta_description: Researchers in Uganda found that catnip oil lotion works as well as DEET for mosquito protection—and costs far less for rural communities.
summary_short: Catnip oil matched DEET's effectiveness in Uganda field trials, offering affordable insect protection for communities that can't afford commercial repellents.
body:
Malaria kills hundreds of thousands each year, mostly children in sub-Saharan Africa. The leading defense—DEET-based repellents—remains out of reach for rural farmers who need it most. That gap may be closing.

Researchers from Cardiff University tested a six percent catnip oil lotion against DEET in eastern Uganda, counting mosquito landings on volunteers' legs over evenings. The results matched. "We found that a six percent catnip oil was just as effective as DEET," said Dr. Simon Scofield, who led the research.

Catnip, a common mint-family herb, contains nepetalactone—the compound that makes cats behave strangely. For years, scientists knew it repelled insects. Nobody had productized it. This trial, presented at the Society for Experimental Biology conference in Florence, changes that.

The real innovation isn't just efficacy. Catnip grows locally in Uganda. The team is now testing whether communities can produce the lotion themselves, creating a self-sustaining supply chain without relying on grants.

One challenge remains: people must actually use the lotion regularly. Compliance with any topical repellent is low. But a cheap, locally made option narrows that friction. For subsistence farmers, that difference could be profound.
